description: REVIEW REQUIRED. LLM-extracted claims and LLM-PROPOSED relationships. Inspect and approve the relationships before running the engine. Reliability is from document metadata, not LLM-judged.

claims.claim_text: The great landmasses of the Earth are not fixed in place but have drifted and continue to drift slowly across the globe over vast geological time.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The continents were once joined together in a single primordial supercontinent that broke apart, with its fragments wandering to their present positions.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The correspondence of opposing coasts, the distribution of fossils, and the matching of geological formations across oceans are signatures of ancient continental displacement, not coincidences.
claims.source: Drifting-continents monograph (1915), dismissed by the geological establishment of the day

claims.claim_text: The permanence of the continents and ocean basins is a settled principle of geology, and continents do not and cannot wander across the Earth's surface.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: No known force is adequate to propel continental masses horizontally through the rigid rock beneath them.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: The occurrence of the same fossil species on lands now separated by ocean is best explained by former land bridges that have since foundered beneath the sea.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: The notion of drifting continents is a fanciful speculation unsupported by any sound mechanism.
claims.source: Authoritative geology textbook, early-20th-century fixist consensus

claims.claim_text: The eastern coastline of South America and the western coastline of Africa mirror each other closely, with the bulge of Brazil corresponding to the inward curve of the Gulf of Guinea.
The geometric fit between the South American and African coastlines is too exact across too great a length to be dismissed as coincidence.
The seed-fern Glossopteris is found in the rocks of South America, Africa, India, and Australia.
Remains of the freshwater reptile Mesosaurus occur on both sides of the South Atlantic.
The presence of land-bound and freshwater species on landmasses now divided by broad oceans is difficult to explain unless those landmasses were formerly contiguous.
The mountain belts and distinctive rock strata of the eastern coast of South America align with those of western Africa, matching in age, sequence, and character.
claims.source: Cross-Atlantic rock-strata and glacial-deposit field notes

claims.claim_text: Ancient glacial deposits of the same period are found across the southern continents in positions that make little sense if the continents were always fixed but fall into order if those lands were once assembled together nearer the pole.
claims.source: Cross-Atlantic rock-strata and glacial-deposit field notes

relationships.rationale: The claim that continents were once joined in a supercontinent and then broke apart and wandered is a direct elaboration and supporting mechanism for the core thesis that continents have drifted across the globe.
The specific geometric observation that Brazil's bulge mirrors the Gulf of Guinea's inward curve is the concrete evidence underpinning the claim that the fit is too exact to be coincidence.

A coastline fit too exact to be coincidental across such great length is direct observational evidence that the continents have moved from a formerly joined position, supporting the core drift thesis.
The detailed mirroring of South American and African coastlines is physical evidence supporting the core claim that continents have drifted from previously joined positions.
The precise geometric fit between the two coastlines supports the claim that they were once part of a single joined landmass.
The close mirroring of South American and African coastlines supports the idea that these landmasses were once contiguous parts of a supercontinent.
The conclusion that the fit is too exact to be coincidence directly supports the claim that coast correspondences are signatures of displacement, not coincidence.
A coastline fit too exact to be coincidental across such a great length directly contradicts the principle that continents are permanently fixed and have never moved.
The occurrence of Glossopteris across four now-separated southern continents is observational evidence that those landmasses were once connected, supporting the core continental drift thesis.
Mesosaurus remains on both sides of the South Atlantic provide direct fossil evidence that those landmasses were once joined, supporting the core drift thesis.
Glossopteris found across South America, Africa, India, and Australia supports the idea of a single former supercontinent from which all these landmasses derived.
Mesosaurus on both sides of the South Atlantic supports the claim that South America and Africa were once part of a joined supercontinent.
The difficulty of explaining land-bound and freshwater species on ocean-divided landmasses without prior contiguity supports the core thesis that those landmasses have drifted apart.
Glossopteris spanning four widely separated southern continents is a concrete instance of land-bound species distribution that is difficult to explain without former contiguity.
Mesosaurus, a freshwater reptile found on both sides of the South Atlantic, is a prime example of a species whose distribution is difficult to explain without former land contiguity.

A freshwater reptile like Mesosaurus could not have crossed a deep ocean via a sunken land bridge, so its presence on both sides of the South Atlantic directly contradicts the land-bridge explanation for trans-oceanic fossil distributions.

The claim that land-bound and freshwater species cannot plausibly have crossed broad oceans even via land bridges directly contradicts the establishment explanation that land bridges account for shared fossil species.
Mesosaurus, a freshwater species, found on both sides of the South Atlantic is inexplicable if continents are permanently fixed, directly contradicting the permanence principle.
Glossopteris spanning four now-separated continents is extremely difficult to explain if those continents are permanently fixed, contradicting the permanence principle.

Mountain belts and rock strata that align in age, sequence, and character across the South Atlantic are physical geological evidence that these continents were once joined and have since drifted apart.
Matching geological formations across the two continents support the claim that South America and Africa were once part of a single supercontinent.
The alignment of mountain belts and strata across oceans is a direct example of a geological formation match that the monograph identifies as a signature of ancient displacement, not coincidence.
Rock strata and mountain belts matching in age, sequence, and character across the South Atlantic directly contradict the principle that continents are fixed and have never been joined.

Ancient glacial deposits spread across southern continents in positions only sensible if those lands were once assembled near the pole is strong evidence for continental drift, supporting the core thesis.
The glacial deposit pattern only makes sense if the southern continents were once assembled together, supporting the claim of a former supercontinent.

Glacial deposits that only make geographical sense if the southern continents were once joined and near the pole directly contradict the claim that continents have always been fixed in their current positions.

Identifying coast correspondences, fossil distributions, and geological formation matches as signatures of displacement rather than coincidence directly supports the core claim that continents have drifted.
The claim that fossil distributions are signatures of actual continental displacement directly contradicts the establishment explanation that they are instead explained by sunken land bridges.
Explaining shared fossil species via sunken land bridges rather than continental movement is an alternative hypothesis that contradicts the core drift thesis.
The permanence principle — that continents cannot wander — is a direct negation of the core drift thesis.
The absence of any known adequate driving force contradicts the claim that continents do drift, by undermining the physical plausibility of the core thesis.
Characterising continental drift as fanciful speculation without sound mechanism is a direct challenge to the core drift thesis.
The absence of a known adequate driving force is the specific mechanistic argument that substantiates the broader characterisation of drift as unsupported speculation.
The settled principle of continental permanence reinforces the establishment's dismissal of drift as fanciful speculation.
Offering the land-bridge explanation as an alternative for fossil distributions supports the establishment's position that drift is unnecessary speculation.
